reasoning: The vulnerable sink (req.method = method.toUpperCase(), line 75) is reachable at default configuration with no non-default options required. The default getter reads the X-HTTP-Method-Override request header, which any HTTP client can set freely. The only guard is the supports() allowlist (lines 140-144), which validates that the supplied value is a known HTTP verb name — but this allowlist explicitly permits DELETE, PUT, PATCH, and other privileged verbs that an attacker would use for method spoofing. There is no authorization check, no rate limiting, and no schema validation within the middleware itself. The header-based attack path is the default configuration: methodOverride() with no arguments already reads X-HTTP-Method-Override from the request headers. An attacker needs only to send a POST request with the header X-HTTP-Method-Override: DELETE (or PUT/PATCH) to cause downstream middleware and route handlers to see req.method === 'DELETE'. If authorization middleware runs downstream of method-override (a common and documented anti-pattern), access controls based on req.method are bypassable. The query string path (_method) requires a non-default getter string, but the header path — which is the default — is fully exploitable without any configuration change. Severity remains medium: the bypass is real and default-reachable, but exploitation requires the consuming application to place authorization checks downstream of method-override middleware.
